sentences.text: My name is Joseph Talpert.
Yes, J-O-S-E-P-H T-A-L-P-E-R-T.
Identical.
No, not at all.
Not so far, no.
My birthdate is 21 September, 1916.
The city is London, and the county is England.
No, my family had to leave Belgium due to the war in 1914, 1918.
They were refugees in London, where they got married, and I was born at that time.
Well, I was til the end of the war.
Must be '18, 1918.
Well, all the refugees went back to the countries where they came.
And my parents, at the time, went back to Belgium.
Well, that was our home country, yeah.
Antwerp.
That's correct.
Well, the area lived was near the municipal park.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, it was a residential area.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, Antwerp is not such a big town, but this was a residential because there's some business areas.
This wasn't exactly in the business area.
It was more residential.
No, we lived in a apartment, a flat.
Well it's usual flat you know?
A few bedrooms, dining room, lounge.
The usual furniture.
I wouldn't say anything particular.
It's the furniture what-- what was the usual-- I wouldn't say anything-- I would have described it-- it was nicely furnished, but I wouldn't say it was extraordinary.
Yes, I had my own room, yes.
I don't think anything in particular.
Well, they were quite a pleasant couple, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just having a bit of a problem.
I don't know what you want to know exactly.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would say they were well matched couple, I would call it loving couple That's all I can say.
Yes, so far, no, yes.
My father was a partner in a clothing factory with two of my uncles.
The factory was in Antwerp.
Well, the street is called [NON-ENGLISH] street.
It was quite a distance in this southern part of Antwerp.
By car.
He had his own car.
Yeah, it was-- Well, you could go-- in Antwerp they quite-- tram service is good and the bus service is good.
Anyway, if not by car, you could always find other means of transport.
Well, we had customers all over the country, all over Belgium, also Luxembourg, but it's a little country on the border.
And we were quite well known and well liked in the country.
Well, he liked the acting part.
In shows, he liked to participate.
Well, it was rather Jewish group.
Jewish acting.
My mother didn't take an act and at the early stage she used to work.
At home, she was a diamond cutter.
Yeah, we had a-- well, in diamond trade, you can have a machine.
She didn't work in a factory.
We had a machine at home, and she used to do a bit of work there.
At the early stage of their marriage.
I don't think so, not particular.
Did you have brothers and sisters?
I had a brother, a little bit younger than me.
Maximilian.
Maxi.
And what was he like?
He was a very pleasant youngster.
We had, rather, yeah.
No, I wouldn't say so.
Well, he had his own activity, and I had my own activity.
I was active in the Zionist [?  ?] movement.
Yeah.
Now, we had a student group.
And I used to take, also, a big interest in the [INAUDIBLE] where I was rather active.
Yes, I had many friends.
Quite a big range of friends.
We used to go out together.
Quite a pleasant life.
Only the war spoiled it.
Well, we used to go and enter-- being on the river [NON-ENGLISH], we used to have those outings.
There was a boat going to Holland.
This was quite a big outing, what we had.
You know, it was quite entertainment.
We got to go a town in Holland called [NON-ENGLISH].
And then we had other outings around Antwerp where we used to go just over the river.
Well, I used to play tennis.
And also, at a later stage, I used to play cards, bridge.
Well, it was quite a pleasant life, you know.
I went, first to municipal school, what was a little distance away from us, and then the [NON-ENGLISH], what they call-- that's the high school.
There were Jewish schools, but I didn't go in that Jewish school.
--was a government school, a municipal school, yeah.
Yes, education was good.
Of course, in Belgium, it's not like in South Africa.
School is the whole day-- finish 4, 5 o'clock in the afternoon.
To be quite candid, I had no particular ambition.
I never made up my mind what I'm going to do.
And then war came, and everything was spoiled.
Well, I wouldn't call it religious, but we were observant-- what I would say, orthodox, conservative.
Well, the Jewish holidays we used to celebrate.
Usually go to shul for the high holidays and you just observed the Jewish holidays.
Yeah, our home was kosher.
Yes, I had a Jewish education til my bar mitzvah.
What I--
--called-- was a type cheder what we had.
In Antwerp.
Yes.
Yeah.
After school.
My what?
Afraid to say my memory is not so good, but I can describe it, because it was combined with a wedding-- my uncle's wedding.
We had it combined.
A brother of my mother.
And the bar mitzvah was combined celebration with their wedding.
Yes, the whole family was there plus quite a lot of guests.
It would be too long to describe because they were quite a lot of people.
You had a big extended family?
Yes.
Your father had brothers and sisters and--
Yes, my father had a brother, two sisters.
And my mother, they had also few sisters and brother.
And then, we had a lot of friends.
Well, originally my family comes from Lithuania, Russia.
Then-- I wouldn't say everybody was there, but at least the close family was there, plus many friends.
It was in a small home in Antwerp.
Yes, my brother was at the bar mitzvah.
Not really, not really.
Well speak about politics, but the truth is they didn't take an active part in politics.
Yes, oh, of course it's everybody.
We were all upset about it, when it started, because the country was put on alert.
Not, I'm afraid, not it help much but anyhow it was an alert already started 1939 already, and everybody was upset.
Yes, there was anti-Semitism in Belgium like in any other country, I would imagine.
There were some Flemish movements what were extreme right, and they were anti-Semitic, but I wouldn't say it was on an unbearable scale.
There was a movement.
Not really.
Not personally.
Yes.
No, they were all mostly Jewish.
I had a few friends who were not Jewish but the majority was Jewish.
No, friends were mostly Jewish.
Here and there there's some business relation where they are not Jewish, but I would say we circulate in circles which were Jewish.
In general, it was good.
I understand that it changed only war time, when I wasn't there.
Then people showed their real colors, their real feelings.
Anti-Semitic feelings-- but it might be caused to some greed, you know, that-- that some people told that Jews has a better life.
And I think this was one of the causes of anti-Semitism in Belgium.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Well, nobody was really happy about the events happening there.
But due to the fact that they had a war before, they never expected things to go to that extreme, because Germany occupied Belgium in 1914, '18, and there was nothing anti-Jewish at the time.
Everybody was worried about the time, but then nobody could expect it would go to that extreme.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
No, they never spoke about leaving.
They spoke about the children-- they perhaps should leave.
When things-- we have family in the United States, and some Jewish families way of thinking about-- not only thinking-- some were sending their children away.
To the United States.
And other countries also, but mainly United States.
Yeah, the parents stayed behind, yes.
When war started?
Well, when war started it was, of course, a-- a big departure immediately.
Most people who could leave, left.
They went to France, because this was the first place, where people talked about going, because we always thought that France was a very strong country, and it would be rather safe there.
Belgium, as the way it went-- we could see that Belgium couldn't resist.
Then there was a huge departure towards France as the war started.
And Belgium was invaded the 10th of May, 1940, and immediately, people started leaving.
In fact, my family and myself we all left at the 10th of May.
Yes
Well, we just closed.
Well, bombardment started, and it didn't sound very good.
Then perhaps that year was that it wouldn't last long.
You know, we'd be able to come back, because we had confidence in the neighboring country, which was mainly France, that they should be able to intervene, and things might improve then.
Very little.
Only bare necessities.
By car.
Yeah, we had a few-- we had my parents.
We had the cars from the business, and we traveled with the cars.
Well, in our car there was, yeah, four.
That's right.
We traveled together with our uncle.
We were with this family also.
We traveled in two cars.
We went towards the north-- well, I would say northwards towards the France-- like Rouen, we went that way-- Bordeaux.
And eventually, in France itself, there were already organization-- French was directing people which direction to go.
Yes, there was quite a lot.
Many cars on the road.
They were all going mainly to France.
Well, there was no other country, because the other side would have been Germany.
There was only one direction you could go.
France, you could go further if you wanted, of course.
No, you didn't need travel documents.
Well, you had your identity paper, your passport and-- but even then, you didn't need to.
Identity papers were sufficient.
It was no problem.
The border was open.
They were just showing it-- a way, where to go.
The French authorities.
Well, when you came to France, they showed you-- they were directing which way to go in France.
They wouldn't show you to go to Paris.
They were showing you to go southwards, towards the south of France
Not exactly, but we were following the road-- the instructions of the of the gendarmerie, the police, the army.
To be quite candid, nobody had particular plans, because nobody expected things to go so bad-- turned much worse than expected.
And besides this, nobody had financial arrangements to go immediately further, because everything was left behind.
At the [INAUDIBLE] when it started in the morning, the banks opened-- was a mad rush.
People taking some money out, but nobody knew how much you had to take out.
Nobody was prepared for it.
Well, in France, we eventually landed in Toulouse.
And there, stayed sometime.
Of course, Belgium was occupied, at the time.
And-- didn't sound so bad.
The rumors were the German behaved well.
They did behave well at the begin.
Then eventually, my parents, my brother, and my-- another part of the family [INAUDIBLE], they went bank to Belgium.
Well, they left everything behind, and they were told things were right to settle matters They needed means, and everything was there.
There was-- had to fix up matters at the factory, had to fix up the home arrangement, everything.
Then they went back.
And the news we could, that there was no problem.
Well, they left me, yes.
I remained Toulouse, at the time.
Because I was a British national.
A British citizen at the time.
Now, I'm South African.
Then it was dangerous for me to go back.
At the time I was 20-- 24.
Well, I had no particular plan.
I didn't know.
I wanted to get out of France.
In fact, [INAUDIBLE] I managed to get a visa.
Military age was already a problem at the time, because military age, they were not allowed to leave.
The German were controlling, already, France.
In the short time they controlled them, France was divided in what they call the Vichy area and occupied France.
In the Vichy area, they had, already, to follow some German instructions.
And one of these instructions, that military men could not leave the country.
In fact, I managed to get a visa for somewhere just to go-- a courtesy visa what was Central America [INAUDIBLE], at the time.
But when I wanted to cross the border, they wouldn't allow me.
Well, at the first stage I wasn't so worried about this, because you used to take information-- information were good.
The authorities gave you information saying there's nothing to worry-- behavior of the German is good.
And there was nothing anti-Jewish said at the time.
Then I wasn't worried.
I had a guilt feeling later-- must be honest-- a guilt feeling from a certain letter that I got from my parents.
And they seemed to blame me that I couldn't tell them what to do.
Now, this was, rather, stage where things were bad.
And I couldn't tell them-- well, even if I would have told them.
First, mail was censored.
You can't write what you want in the mail.
And-- and, well, I really thought they should have left Belgium, but I couldn't write so-- And it wasn't easy to leave at the time already.
Control was severe.
And in fact, some of my family from the States tried to get them out.
They booked them, even, a passage from Lisbon on a Portuguese boat to go to the States.
They made all the arrangements, but unfortunately they couldn't leave.
Officially, they couldn't leave unless they managed to smuggle out of the-- I suppose they couldn't find a way for doing it.
Some people managed, some didn't manage.
Very few managed to leave the country at that time, because everything was controlled, checked by the Germans.
And you used to pay people, you know.
There were-- what you call-- people who try to get you out, but this didn't work always.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In fact, one of my auntie's and their family-- they also arranged with somebody to take them to Switzerland.
They were caught on the way.
